:::{#def-interval-fail-prob}
#### Interval failure probability

Fix an interval width $\Delta t$ and grid points $\tau$.
For subject $j$, the **interval failure probability** at $\tau$ is the
conditional probability that $j$ fails during $[\tau, \tau + \Delta t)$,
given that $j$ is still at risk at $\tau$:

$$
q_j(\tau) \eqdef \Pr\sb{j \text{ fails in } [\tau,\, \tau + \Delta t) \mid j \text{ at risk at } \tau}.
$$
:::

:::{#lem-cond-surv-ratio}
#### Conditional survival as a ratio

For $\tau_1 < \tau_2$, the conditional probability of surviving past $\tau_2$
given survival past $\tau_1$ is the ratio of survival function values:

$$
\Pr(T > \tau_2 \mid T > \tau_1) = \frac{\surv(\tau_2)}{\surv(\tau_1)}.
$$ {#eq-cond-surv-ratio}
:::

{{< slidebreak >}}

::: {.proof}
Since $\tau_2 > \tau_1$, the event $\{T > \tau_2\}$ is a subset of $\{T > \tau_1\}$,
so their intersection is just $\{T > \tau_2\}$
([subset property](probability.qmd#thm-prob-subset)).
By the [definition of conditional probability](probability.qmd#def-conditional-prob),

$$
\ba
\Pr(T > \tau_2 \mid T > \tau_1)
&= \frac{\Pr(T > \tau_2,\ T > \tau_1)}{\Pr(T > \tau_1)}
&& \text{(definition of conditional probability)}
\\
&= \frac{\Pr(T > \tau_2)}{\Pr(T > \tau_1)}
&& \text{(}\{T > \tau_2\} \subseteq \{T > \tau_1\}\text{)}
\\
&= \frac{\surv(\tau_2)}{\surv(\tau_1)} .
&& \text{(definition of the survival function)}
\ea
$$
:::

:::{#exm-cond-surv-ratio}
#### Conditional survival ratio for an exponential lifetime

Let $T \sim \mathrm{Exponential}(\lambda)$, so $\surv(t) = \expf{-\lambda t}$.
The probability of surviving past $\tau_2$ given survival past $\tau_1 < \tau_2$ is

$$
\ba
\Pr(T > \tau_2 \mid T > \tau_1)
&= \frac{\surv(\tau_2)}{\surv(\tau_1)}
&& \text{(conditional survival ratio)}
\\
&= \frac{\expf{-\lambda \tau_2}}{\expf{-\lambda \tau_1}}
&& \text{(substitute the exponential survival function)}
\\
&= \expf{-\lambda(\tau_2 - \tau_1)} ,
&& \text{(quotient of exponentials)}
\ea
$$

which depends only on the elapsed time $\tau_2 - \tau_1$, not on $\tau_1$ itself —
the memoryless property of the exponential distribution.
:::

:::{#exm-interval-fail-prob}
#### Interval failure probability: exact value

Suppose subject $j$ has constant hazard $\haz(t) = 0.1$ per year,
and consider the half-year interval $[4.0,\, 4.5)$, so $\Delta t = 0.5$ years.
By @lem-cond-surv-ratio and the survival/cumulative-hazard relationship (@cor-surv-int-haz),
the exact probability that $j$ fails in this interval, given survival to year $4$, is

$$
\ba
q_j(4.0)
&= \Pr\sb{j \text{ fails in } [4.0,\, 4.5) \mid j \text{ at risk at } 4.0}
&& \text{(definition of } q_j \text{)}
\\
&= 1 - \Pr\sb{j \text{ survives past } 4.5 \mid j \text{ at risk at } 4.0}
&& \text{(complement rule)}
\\
&= 1 - \frac{\surv(4.5)}{\surv(4.0)}
&& \text{(conditional survival ratio)}
\\
&= 1 - \frac{\expf{-\int_{0}^{4.5} \haz(u)\,du}}
            {\expf{-\int_{0}^{4.0} \haz(u)\,du}}
&& \text{(survival from cumulative hazard)}
\\
&= 1 - \expf{-\int_{4.0}^{4.5} \haz(u)\,du}
&& \text{(quotient of exponentials)}
\\
&= 1 - \expf{-\int_{4.0}^{4.5} 0.1 \,du}
&& \text{(substitute } \haz(u) = 0.1\text{)}
\\
&= 1 - \expf{-0.1 \times 0.5}
&& \text{(constant integrand over width } \Delta t = 0.5\text{)}
\\
&= 1 - \expf{-0.05}
&& \text{(arithmetic)}
\\
&\approx 0.0488 .
&& \text{(evaluate } e^{-0.05} \approx 0.9512\text{)}
\ea
$$
:::
